title: SEGMENT 13 — Super-resolution: turning glow sticks on one at a time

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Super-resolution: turning glow sticks on one at a time

Now we get to one of the most consequential capabilities to come out of fluorescent protein engineering: super-resolution microscopy, the family of techniques that broke a physical limit that had stood for over a century.
This earned the 2014 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, and fluorescent proteins played a central role.
Let me set up the problem.
Way back in the 1870s, a German physicist named Ernst Abbe worked out that you cannot resolve two points of light that are closer together than about half the wavelength of the light you're using.
For visible light at 500 nanometers, that means the smallest features a microscope can distinguish are about 200 to 250 nanometers apart.
If two objects are 100 nanometers apart, no matter how good your lenses are, they blur into a single spot.
That's the diffraction limit.
For over a century, the diffraction limit was treated as an unbreakable physical law.
It's not a limitation of glass or money — it's a limit imposed by the wave nature of light itself.
And it's an annoying limit, because lots of important biology lives below 200 nanometers.
Single ribosomes are about 25 nanometers.
Nuclear pore complexes are about 100 nanometers.
Synaptic structures are tens of nanometers.
All of those are dark, blurry blobs under a conventional fluorescence microscope.
Then, in the 1990s and 2000s, a series of remarkably clever ideas showed that you could effectively get around the diffraction limit — not by violating the physics, but by being clever about time.
Here's the key insight, and it's beautiful.
A single fluorescent molecule, by itself, also produces a 200-nanometer blob in the microscope — the same diffraction-limited blur.
But that blob is centered on the true position of the molecule.
If you collect enough photons from that single molecule, you can fit the blob mathematically and find its center with much higher precision than the blob's width.
With a few thousand photons from one molecule, you can locate it to within a few nanometers.
The precision of locating a single point is much better than the resolution of distinguishing two separate points.
So the trick becomes: can you arrange for there to be only one fluorescent molecule lit up at a time in any given diffraction-limited region?
Then you can localize each one precisely.
The catch, of course, is that real cellular structures — a microtubule decorated with thousands of fluorescent proteins, say — have far too many molecules close together.
They overlap.
You can't separate them.
The solution: cycle them.
Use a switchable fluorescent protein.
Most of the molecules sit in the dark state.
A weak pulse of activating light flips a tiny random subset into the bright state.
You image them — the few sparse molecules — fit each one to find its precise center, then those molecules bleach away.
Another pulse flips a different small random subset on.
Image, localize, bleach.
Repeat many thousands of cycles.

After enough rounds, you have an enormous list of precise localizations, and you can build a super-resolution image from the list — a pointillist painting made of tens of thousands of precisely-placed dots.
The analogy I love is: imagine you're standing on a hill, trying to map a city at night.
If all the streetlights are on at once, the city is a blur — too many lights too close together.

But if you can turn on just one streetlight at a time and precisely note its location, then turn it off and turn on a different one, eventually you can build up a precise map of every streetlight in the city.
That's what super-resolution does, except the streetlights are individual fluorescent molecules and the city is the inside of a cell.
That principle is the foundation of two techniques developed in 2006.
PALM — photoactivated localization microscopy, from Eric Betzig — uses photoactivatable or photoconvertible fluorescent proteins for the switching.
The mEos family, which I mentioned in Segment 9, is the workhorse.
You tag your protein with mEos, you express it in cells.
You hit it with weak pulses of violet light that convert a small random subset from green to red.
You image the red, localize each spot precisely, those molecules bleach.
Repeat thousands of times.
You assemble an image with about 20-nanometer resolution — ten times better than the diffraction limit.
STORM — stochastic optical reconstruction microscopy, from Xiaowei Zhuang — uses the same principle but with small-molecule dyes instead of proteins.
Certain dyes, particularly cyanine dyes like Cy5 or Alexa Fluor 647, can be made to blink between bright and dark states under specific buffer conditions.
The blinking gives the same single-molecule sparsity, and the same localization-and-reconstruction trick applies.
There's a third super-resolution technique, STED — stimulated emission depletion, from Stefan Hell — that works differently.
STED uses a second laser shaped like a doughnut to actively shut off the fluorescence everywhere except the center hole of the doughnut.
That effectively shrinks the excitation spot below the diffraction limit.
It's a different physical trick but it gets you to similar resolutions, and it works with normal fluorescent proteins.
Betzig, Hell, and W.
E.
Moerner shared the 2014 Nobel.

The take-home for fluorescent proteins: super-resolution opened up an entirely new scale of biology, the molecular scale at ten to one hundred nanometers, where individual proteins and complexes live.

Discoveries that came out of super-resolution include the discrete arrangement of receptors in synapses, the gorgeous actin-spectrin ring scaffold inside the axons of neurons, the detailed architecture of bacterial cell division machinery, and many more.
And the photoactivatable and photoconvertible proteins are essential reagents for PALM — they're not optional accessories.
The engineering of switchable fluorescent proteins and the optics of super-resolution came together to deliver an entirely new class of microscopy.
It is genuinely magical that you can now see, in cells, structures that the founders of light microscopy considered fundamentally invisible.
